stem:
A 24-year-old male medical student presents to a metropolitan emergency department six hours after a hollow-bore needlestick injury sustained while clearing a sharps container. The source patient is a 38-year-old man known to be living with HIV; his most recent viral load three months ago was reported as undetectable on bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide and his HIV physician has been contactable.

The needle had visible fresh blood and entered the dorsum of the student's left hand to a depth of around 3 mm with active bleeding. The student washed the wound under running water and the injury was reported within 10 minutes. His baseline HIV serology, hepatitis B surface antibody (vaccinated, anti-HBs 240), hepatitis C antibody, and pregnancy considerations are not applicable. He has no current medications.

data_table.Investigations: source patient HIV viral load undetectable 3 months ago, source patient hepatitis B surface antigen negative, source patient hepatitis C antibody negative, student baseline HIV serology pending, anti-HBs 240 mIU/mL (>10 protective), student hepatitis C antibody pending, full blood count and renal function within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding HIV post exposure prophylaxis?

options.text: Reassure that the source undetectable viral load makes PEP unnecessary and arrange follow up serology at 6 weeks and 3 months
options.rationale: Undetectable viral load on treatment for at least six months strongly reduces transmission risk but the ASHM guideline still recommends offering PEP for high risk percutaneous needlestick exposures unless source recent viral load is documented undetectable AND adherence is verified. Documented adherence requires a more recent check than three months. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Commence 5 days of bictegravir, emtricitabine, and tenofovir alafenamide and review at day 5 to decide whether to continue
options.rationale: PEP duration is 28 days as a fixed course; truncated regimens have not been shown to prevent infection. The chosen regimen is also not the ASHM first line PEP combination. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Commence 28 days of zidovudine plus lamivudine as a two drug regimen following older Australian and overseas guidance
options.rationale: Two drug PEP is no longer standard; three drug regimens are preferred for high risk percutaneous exposures because of improved tolerability and resistance profile. Zidovudine is also no longer first line in Australian PEP regimens. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Commence 28 days of tenofovir 245 mg, emtricitabine 200 mg, and raltegravir 400 mg twice daily within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: Hollow bore percutaneous injury with visible blood from a known HIV positive source meets ASHM high risk criteria. Three drug PEP is recommended: tenofovir/emtricitabine backbone plus an integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir) for 28 days, starting within 72 hours (sooner is better; pharmacokinetic effect drops sharply after 48 to 72 hours). (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Defer PEP pending source patient viral load on the same day and start only if the result is detectable above 200 copies
options.rationale: Waiting for results delays PEP initiation past the highly time sensitive window. PEP is started immediately and discontinued if the source is later confirmed HIV negative or genuinely undetectable with documented adherence. (Source: ASHM National PEP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) in Australia follows ASHM guidelines. High risk exposures (percutaneous injury with hollow needle and visible blood from a known HIV source, receptive anal intercourse with HIV positive source) warrant three drug PEP for 28 days within 72 hours; tenofovir/emtricitabine plus an integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir) is the preferred backbone. Baseline HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, syphilis, and pregnancy testing where applicable are arranged. Follow up HIV serology at 6 weeks and 3 months (4th generation antigen-antibody) confirms or excludes seroconversion. Counselling on safer sex and abstinence from blood donation until cleared is essential.
key_points: Start PEP within 72 hours (earlier the better), continue for 28 days
Three drug regimen: tenofovir + emtricitabine + integrase inhibitor (raltegravir or dolutegravir)
Baseline HIV, hepatitis B and C, syphilis, pregnancy test where applicable
Follow up 4th generation HIV serology at 6 weeks and 3 months
Discontinue PEP if source confirmed HIV negative or genuinely durably undetectable
explanation.pearls: Source virological suppression (durable undetectable on ART for at least 6 months with documented adherence) reduces sexual transmission risk to effectively zero (U=U) but for high risk percutaneous needlestick the residual uncertainty about source adherence usually still warrants PEP unless real-time viral load can be confirmed.

stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.4 percent on metformin and gliclazide) presents to a regional emergency department with 36 hours of progressive right calf pain and swelling after he caught his ankle on a fence post. The redness is now spreading rapidly above the knee. He says the pain is severe, much worse than the appearance suggests.

On examination he is unwell-appearing with rigors. The right calf is erythematous extending above the knee with a 12 cm patch of dusky purple skin, two small superficial blisters, and exquisite tenderness extending well beyond the visible margin of erythema. Sensation is reduced over part of the dusky area, and there is no crepitus. Vital signs are below. Bedside investigations are obtained.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 124, blood pressure 102/62, respiratory rate 24, SpO2 96 percent on room air, temperature 38.6 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 124 g/L, white cells 22.4 x10^9/L, sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), glucose 17.4 mmol/L, creatinine 162 micromol/L, CRP 286 mg/L (<5), venous lactate 4.2 mmol/L, LRINEC score 9, plain radiograph of leg without subcutaneous gas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Mark the leading edge of erythema, admit for intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 6 hours, and review the leg in 12 hours
options.rationale: LRINEC score of 9 (high probability necrotising fasciitis), pain out of proportion, dusky skin with blistering, and systemic features mandate urgent surgical exploration and broad spectrum antibiotics. Monitoring and narrow spectrum therapy alone is inadequate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer high dose intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as the first intervention before surgical exploration of the leg
options.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome where supported, used after source control and broad spectrum antibiotics, not as the first intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g every 4 hours alone and arrange magnetic resonance imaging before any surgery
options.rationale: MRI delays definitive treatment and necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis supported by surgical exploration. Benzylpenicillin alone is inadequate empirical cover. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Apply hyperbaric oxygen therapy as the primary treatment without immediate surgical debridement by the on call surgical team
options.rationale: Hyperbaric oxygen is adjunctive (where available) after surgical debridement, not a substitute for it. Delay risks rapid progression and mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Take blood cultures, give intravenous meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin, and arrange urgent surgical debridement
options.rationale: LRINEC 6 or more raises the probability of necrotising fasciitis to greater than 50 percent and clinical features here (pain out of proportion, dusky/bullous skin, sodium 132, lactate 4.2, CRP 286) all support the diagnosis. Empirical broad spectrum coverage including a beta lactam (meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam), vancomycin for MRSA, and clindamycin for toxin suppression is standard, with simultaneous surgical debridement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection is a surgical emergency with mortality 25 to 35 percent. The LRINEC score (CRP, white cells, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, glucose) stratifies probability but does not replace clinical judgement; pain out of proportion, dusky discoloration, bullae, crepitus, and rapidly spreading erythema are red flags. Treatment is simultaneous urgent surgical debridement with empirical broad spectrum antibiotics (meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus clindamycin for toxin suppression). IVIG is considered for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome.
key_points: Pain out of proportion to appearance is the classical early sign
LRINEC 6 or more: > 50 percent probability of necrotising fasciitis
Empirical: meropenem (or piperacillin-tazobactam) + vancomycin + clindamycin
Urgent surgical debridement is the cornerstone
Clindamycin added for toxin suppression in streptococcal disease
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin 600 mg IV every 8 hours suppresses streptococcal exotoxin production and continues for at least 48 hours even after surgical source control - the antitoxin benefit is independent of cell wall activity.

stem:
A 64-year-old man with longstanding hypertension and bicuspid aortic valve known to a cardiologist presents to a regional emergency department with two hours of sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the interscapular area and now to the right loin. He says the pain was maximal at onset. He has not had recent trauma and has not used cocaine. He has had no syncope. He is a non-smoker.

On examination he is diaphoretic and frightened. The right radial pulse is weak; the left is normal. Right and left blood pressures differ by 28 mmHg. There is an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge that he says is new (last cardiology echo six months ago noted aortic regurgitation graded 'trivial'). There is no focal neurology, no abdominal tenderness, and palpable femoral pulses bilaterally. There is no clinical pneumothorax. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 102, blood pressure 188/96 right arm and 160/82 left arm, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, troponin within normal limits, D-dimer 6.2 mg/L (<0.5), chest radiograph mediastinal widening, point of care echo dilated ascending aorta with intimal flap

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Intravenous esmolol infusion targeting heart rate 60 to 80 and systolic blood pressure 100 to 120, with immediate CT angiography
options.rationale: Type A aortic dissection (ascending aorta involvement on echo, mediastinal widening, asymmetric pulses, new diastolic murmur) is a surgical emergency. Pre-operative care targets heart rate first with esmolol or labetalol to reduce shear stress (dP/dt), then vasodilator if needed for blood pressure. CT aortic angiography confirms anatomy and guides surgical planning. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion at 50 micrograms per minute to lower blood pressure as the priority intervention
options.rationale: Vasodilators alone increase reflex tachycardia and shear stress on the aorta, which can extend the dissection. Heart rate is controlled with a beta blocker first before adding a vasodilator. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous heparin 80 units/kg bolus then infusion for presumed pulmonary embolus given the markedly elevated D-dimer
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated and catastrophic in aortic dissection; the elevated D-dimer reflects the dissection itself, not pulmonary embolism. CTA must precede any anticoagulation when dissection is suspected. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases Guidelines)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg with morphine 5 mg intravenously and admit for cardiac telemetry observation overnight
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies because of unpredictable hypotension. Observation without surgical referral for type A dissection has greater than 50 percent mortality at 48 hours. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases Guidelines)

options.text: Intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 90 minutes for suspected ST elevation myocardial infarction
options.rationale: Thrombolysis in aortic dissection causes catastrophic haemorrhage. The ECG here shows no ischaemic changes and the clinical picture is dissection, not infarct. (Source: ESC Aortic Diseases Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection is classified by Stanford as type A (ascending aorta or arch, always surgical) or type B (descending aorta, usually medical unless complicated by malperfusion, rupture, or persistent pain). Pre-operative management targets dP/dt: heart rate first to 60 to 80 with esmolol or labetalol, then blood pressure to systolic 100 to 120 with vasodilator if needed. CT aortic angiography is the imaging gold standard; transoesophageal echocardiography is an alternative in unstable patients. Mortality is 1 to 2 percent per hour untreated for type A. Predisposing factors include hypertension, bicuspid aortic valve, Marfan and Loeys-Dietz syndromes, family history, and stimulant use.
key_points: Stanford A (ascending): surgical emergency, 1 to 2 percent mortality per hour untreated
Stanford B (descending): medical unless complicated
Heart rate first (esmolol/labetalol) target 60 to 80, then BP target 100 to 120 systolic
Vasodilators alone are dangerous (reflex tachycardia)
CT aortic angiography is the imaging gold standard
explanation.pearls: Untreated Stanford A aortic dissection mortality rises by 1 to 2 percent per hour for the first 48 hours - the 'time is aorta' window is similar in magnitude to STEMI 'time is muscle' but the intervention is operative repair rather than reperfusion.

stem:
A 36-year-old man is brought to the resuscitation bay of a major trauma centre 15 minutes after a high-speed motorcycle collision. He has bilateral femoral shaft fractures with active bleeding from the right thigh stump (a traumatic above-knee amputation), an open pelvic fracture, and a haemoperitoneum on focused ultrasound (FAST positive). He has had 1 unit of group O red cells from the trauma fridge en route.

On arrival he is alert but confused and pale. Pulse rate 142, blood pressure 76/48, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 92 percent on 10 L oxygen via Hudson mask, temperature 35.4 degrees. Two large bore intravenous cannulae are in. The Code Crimson team has been activated. Initial bloods are returning. The trauma team consultant asks you to coordinate the haematology side of the resuscitation.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 142, blood pressure 76/48, respiratory rate 32, SpO2 92 percent on 10 L oxygen, temperature 35.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: haemoglobin 64 g/L, platelets 122 x10^9/L, INR 1.4, fibrinogen 1.2 g/L (2 to 4), lactate 6.8 mmol/L, base excess negative 8, ionised calcium 0.92 mmol/L (1.10 to 1.30), ROTEM EXTEM CT 95 sec (38 to 79) and FIBTEM A10 6 mm (>10), shock index 1.9

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Give recombinant factor VII 90 micrograms/kg as the primary intervention to control bleeding before any other product
options.rationale: Recombinant factor VIIa is not first line; it is a rescue agent considered after ratio-balanced transfusion, fibrinogen replacement, surgical control, and correction of acidosis/hypocalcaemia. Routine use has not improved mortality and may increase thrombosis. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Protocol)

options.text: Transfuse two units of packed red cells, then reassess clinical status and laboratory results before further blood products
options.rationale: Sequential reassessment under-resuscitates in established trauma induced coagulopathy and risks worsening the acidosis-hypothermia-coagulopathy triad. Activation of the massive transfusion protocol is required when there are signs of ongoing significant bleeding. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Protocol)

options.text: Give 2 L of crystalloid resuscitation before any blood products to restore peripheral perfusion and blood pressure
options.rationale: Crystalloid resuscitation in major haemorrhage dilutes clotting factors, worsens acidosis, increases bleeding, and is contraindicated as the primary resuscitation fluid. Damage control resuscitation prioritises blood products. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Protocol)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol at a 1:1:1 ratio with intravenous tranexamic acid, cryoprecipitate, and calcium
options.rationale: Trauma induced coagulopathy with hypothermia, acidosis, ionised hypocalcaemia, and low fibrinogen (Code Crimson criteria) needs simultaneous correction. The Australian National Blood Authority massive transfusion protocol uses balanced 1:1:1 ratios, TXA within 3 hours, calcium replacement to maintain ionised calcium > 1.1, and goal-directed fibrinogen replacement to keep fibrinogen above 1.5 to 2 g/L (or FIBTEM A10 > 7 mm). (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Protocol)

options.text: Defer tranexamic acid and rely on the trauma surgeon to achieve operative haemostasis before any antifibrinolytic agent
options.rationale: CRASH-2 trial showed tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of trauma reduces all-cause mortality (number needed to treat about 67) and death from bleeding (NNT about 121). Delaying TXA beyond 3 hours loses the benefit and may increase thrombosis. (Source: NBA Massive Transfusion Protocol)

explanation.summary: Massive transfusion in trauma is guided by the National Blood Authority criteria: anticipated requirement of 4 or more units of packed red cells in one hour, or ongoing major bleeding with haemodynamic instability. Australian protocols use balanced 1:1:1 ratios (packed red cells, FFP, platelets), TXA within 3 hours (CRASH-2), goal-directed fibrinogen replacement (cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate, target above 1.5 to 2 g/L), calcium supplementation (ionised calcium > 1.1), prevention of hypothermia, and concurrent surgical or interventional radiology source control. ROTEM or thromboelastography guides component therapy.
key_points: Activate MTP for anticipated 4+ units packed red cells in 1 hour or ongoing major bleeding
1:1:1 ratio (RBC:FFP:platelets) until ROTEM/TEG guidance available
TXA 1 g IV bolus + 1 g over 8 hours, within 3 hours of injury (CRASH-2)
Fibrinogen target > 1.5 to 2 g/L (cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate)
Ionised calcium > 1.1 mmol/L; avoid hypothermia and acidosis
explanation.pearls: Every unit of citrated packed red cells lowers ionised calcium - after 4 units, give 10 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate (or 10 mL of 10 percent calcium chloride via central access) to keep ionised calcium above 1.1 mmol/L and preserve clotting cascade function.

stem:
A 52-year-old woman with poorly adherent hypertension (intermittent perindopril and amlodipine over years) is brought to the emergency department with a 12 hour history of a generalised throbbing headache, two episodes of vomiting, and progressive blurred vision. She has noticed a yellow tinge to lights and difficulty reading texts. There is no focal weakness, no chest pain, and no dyspnoea.

On examination she is alert but uncomfortable. The optic discs are bilaterally swollen with retinal haemorrhages and cotton wool spots. Auscultation reveals mild bibasal crackles without overt fluid overload. Cardiac auscultation is normal. There is no abdominal aortic mass, no carotid bruit, and no focal neurology. Vital signs and bedside investigations are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 92, blood pressure 232/138, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 96 percent on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees
data_table.Investigations: 12 lead ECG sinus rhythm with voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy, troponin within normal limits, urinalysis blood 2+ protein 2+, creatinine 132 micromol/L (baseline 92), CT brain within normal limits, chest radiograph mild pulmonary venous congestion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of her blood pressure in the first hour?

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion at 50 micrograms/min targeting a blood pressure under 140/90 within thirty minutes
options.rationale: Rapid reduction to under 140/90 within 30 minutes risks cerebral and coronary hypoperfusion in chronically hypertensive patients whose autoregulation has shifted. The target is a controlled 20 to 25 percent fall over the first hour. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg and admit for cardiac telemetry observation with reassessment of blood pressure overnight
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in hypertensive emergency because of unpredictable hypotension, reflex tachycardia, and risk of ischaemic stroke. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion targeting mean arterial pressure reduction of about 20 percent over the first 60 minutes
options.rationale: Hypertensive emergency (severe hypertension plus end organ damage: papilloedema/retinopathy, acute kidney injury, mild pulmonary venous congestion) requires controlled lowering. Australian and ESC guidance limits initial mean arterial pressure reduction to 20 to 25 percent in the first hour and to around 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours to avoid cerebral hypoperfusion. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Restart perindopril 5 mg and amlodipine 10 mg orally and review blood pressure response in twenty four hours' time
options.rationale: Oral therapy alone is too slow for hypertensive emergency with end organ damage. Intravenous therapy with continuous monitoring is required for at least the first 24 hours. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Intravenous hydralazine 20 mg bolus repeated every 30 minutes targeting diastolic blood pressure under 70 mmHg today
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes unpredictable hypotension and reflex tachycardia, and the target diastolic of under 70 risks coronary hypoperfusion in left ventricular hypertrophy. Labetalol or a nicardipine/clevidipine infusion are preferred where available. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

explanation.summary: Hypertensive emergency is severe hypertension (typically systolic above 180 or diastolic above 120 mmHg) plus end organ damage (hypertensive encephalopathy, retinopathy with papilloedema, acute kidney injury, acute pulmonary oedema, aortic dissection, acute coronary syndrome, eclampsia). Australian guidance is to reduce mean arterial pressure by 20 to 25 percent over the first hour, then to about 160/100 over 2 to 6 hours, then gradually to normal over 24 to 48 hours. Specific situations have specific targets (aortic dissection systolic under 120, acute ischaemic stroke under 185/110 if thrombolysis, eclampsia under 160/110). First line agents are intravenous labetalol, nicardipine, or clevidipine; glyceryl trinitrate when pulmonary oedema or ACS dominates.
key_points: Hypertensive emergency = severe HT + end organ damage
Target 20 to 25 percent MAP reduction in first hour
Avoid sublingual nifedipine and rapid normalisation
Labetalol, nicardipine, clevidipine are first line
Specific targets: aortic dissection systolic < 120; stroke < 185/110 if thrombolysis; eclampsia < 160/110
explanation.pearls: In hypertensive emergency without aortic dissection, lowering mean arterial pressure below 20 to 25 percent of the presenting value in the first hour can cross the autoregulation lower limit (typically 60 to 70 mmHg MAP in normotensives but shifted up by 20 to 30 mmHg in chronic hypertensives) and precipitate watershed ischaemic stroke.

subtopic_detail: Adrenal insufficiency and perioperative steroid cover
stem:
A 58-year-old woman with autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) for 15 years on hydrocortisone 20 mg morning, 10 mg lunch, and fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily is scheduled for an open Hartmann procedure for diverticular perforation under general anaesthesia. She arrives in the ED via ambulance hypotensive after one day of vomiting, fever, and abdominal pain. She missed her morning hydrocortisone today.

On arrival she is drowsy and pale. Capillary blood glucose 3.2 mmol/L. Pulse rate 132, blood pressure 78/48, respiratory rate 28, SpO2 96 percent on 4 L oxygen via nasal prongs, temperature 38.4 degrees. The abdomen is rigid with rebound tenderness. CT shows free intraperitoneal air and free fluid consistent with perforated diverticulitis. The surgical team plans theatre as soon as she is stabilised. Bloods are below.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse rate 132, blood pressure 78/48, respiratory rate 28, SpO2 96 percent on 4 L oxygen, temperature 38.4 degrees
data_table.Investigations: sodium 128 mmol/L (135 to 145), potassium 5.4 mmol/L (3.5 to 5.0), urea 12.4 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, white cells 22.4 x10^9/L, haemoglobin 124 g/L, CT free intraperitoneal air, blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus then 200 mg over 24 hours, saline resuscitation, antibiotics, and proceed to theatre
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis precipitated by sepsis and surgical illness needs immediate parenteral hydrocortisone (100 mg IV bolus then 200 mg over 24 hours by infusion or 50 mg six hourly), aggressive fluid resuscitation, glucose correction, treatment of the precipitant (broad spectrum antibiotics, surgical source control), and continuation of stress dose hydrocortisone perioperatively. Mineralocorticoid replacement is unnecessary at hydrocortisone doses above 50 mg/day because of cross-binding. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Double her usual oral hydrocortisone dose, give intravenous fluids, and proceed to theatre once vital signs improve
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inadequate during vomiting, sepsis, and impending major surgery; absorption is unreliable and underdosing precipitates further crisis. Parenteral stress dose hydrocortisone is mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 4 mg as the steroid of choice in adrenal crisis given its longer half life than hydrocortisone
options.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks mineralocorticoid activity and is reserved for the diagnostic phase (does not interfere with cortisol assays). Hydrocortisone is preferred for treatment because it provides both glucocorticoid and at high doses sufficient mineralocorticoid activity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Withhold steroids until a random cortisol level can be measured and a short Synacthen test arranged through endocrine
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established (15 year history of Addison disease). Withholding hydrocortisone for repeat testing during a life threatening crisis is dangerous and unnecessary. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous fludrocortisone 200 micrograms as the priority before hydrocortisone for mineralocorticoid replacement now
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is only available orally and has no role during acute crisis; hydrocortisone at stress doses provides adequate mineralocorticoid activity. The acute priority is intravenous hydrocortisone. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency with mortality 6 to 15 percent in known Addison disease, precipitated by intercurrent illness, surgery, missed doses, or new corticosteroid antagonists. Immediate management is intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus then 200 mg over 24 hours (infusion or divided 50 mg six hourly), 1 L 0.9 percent saline in the first hour, glucose correction, identification and treatment of the precipitant, and continuation of stress dose steroids until clinical recovery. Major surgery requires 100 mg pre-induction, then 200 mg over 24 hours; minor surgery requires double the usual dose for 24 hours. Mineralocorticoid replacement (fludrocortisone) resumes only when hydrocortisone falls below 50 mg/day.
key_points: Diagnose adrenal crisis on clinical grounds; do not delay treatment for testing in established disease
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus then 200 mg over 24 hours (infusion or 50 mg six hourly)
1 L 0.9 percent saline in first hour; correct glucose with dextrose if hypoglycaemic
Major surgery: 100 mg pre-induction, 200 mg/24 h until oral intake
Fludrocortisone unnecessary while hydrocortisone > 50 mg/day
explanation.pearls: Australian Addison Disease emergency cards specify an intramuscular hydrocortisone 100 mg dose kept by the patient for self administration before transfer to hospital - the time-critical step that prevents death during gastroenteritis when oral steroids cannot be absorbed.
